coredata.dc:title: Recyclability and selective fluorescence/colorimetric sensing properties of fluorescent porous materials synthesized by the copolymerization of 4-vinylpyridine zinc and divinylbenzene
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, lili
Huang, Yuanliang
Xiong, Yang
Zhang, Quanli
Yang, Junxiao
Huang, Yawen
coredata.dc:description: One of the main drawbacks of porous coordination polymers is their low stability and nonrecyclability in acidic or alkaline environments. Herein, we proposed a “coordination-copolymerization” strategy and successfully introduced covalent crosslinked skeleton into a coordination skeleton via copolymerization of di(4-vinylpyridine) zinc chloride and divinylbenzene, thus producing a polymer having a hybridized skeleton (poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB)). This polymer exhibits a porous structure and fluorescence and colorimetric sensing abilities toward Cu2+ ions. More importantly, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) exhibits high skeleton stability in acidic and alkaline environments. By reintroducing zinc ions, the chemical properties, porous structure, and the sensing ability are regenerated after being destroyed in an acidic or alkaline environment, thus demonstrating its recyclability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous
Fluorescent
Sensing ability
Recyclability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) FTIR and b) XRD spectra of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) and poly(ZnVP2).
photographs of a) poly(ZnVP2) and b) poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) in aqueous solution varying pH.
FTIR spectra of a) pristine poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB); b) that treated with acids and alkalines; c) recovered poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB).
SEM images of a) poly(ZnVP2) and b) poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) TEM images of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB).
N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for poly(ZnVP2) and poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB).

a) photographs of fluorescence emission change (under UV lamp with λex = 312 nm) of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) with CuSO4 aqueous solution of different concentrations; b) The decrease of fluorescent intensity in Cu2+ concentration range of 0∼300 ppm for poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) in water (λex = 312 nm).

a) photographs of fluorescence emission change (under UV lamp of 312 nm) of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) in water in the presence of different metal ions; b) Quenching efficiency of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) in water in the presence of different metal ions.

Ion-responsive properties of Poly(ZnVP2-DVB): a) Quenching efficiency of poly(ZnVP2) in water in the presence of different metal ions (red) and upon addition of Cu2+; b) chromic phenomenon in the presence of different metal ions and upon the addition of Cu2+.
FTIR spectra of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) and that after the uptake of Cu2+.
SEM images of a) pristine poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB); b) that after treated at pH = 2; c) that after recycling.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption curved of a) Poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB), b) after treated at pH = 2, and c) that recycling for three times.
fluorescence emission change in the presence of different metal ions. a) Poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB), b) after treated at pH = 2, and c) that recycling for three times.
Strategy to prepare POP/PCP hybridized polymer and poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB).
Comparison of detection limit of various adsorbents for Cu2+ ion.

head.ce:title: Recyclability and selective fluorescence/colorimetric sensing properties of fluorescent porous materials synthesized by the copolymerization of 4-vinylpyridine zinc and divinylbenzene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Environmental-friendly Energy Materials, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Environmental-friendly Energy Materials
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Environmental-friendly Energy Materials, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang 621010, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, Sichuan 621010, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Southwest University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Southwest University of Science and Technology, Mianyang, Sichuan 621010, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the main drawbacks of porous coordination polymers is their low stability and nonrecyclability in acidic or alkaline environments. Herein, we proposed a “coordination-copolymerization” strategy and successfully introduced covalent crosslinked skeleton into a coordination skeleton via copolymerization of di(4-vinylpyridine) zinc chloride and divinylbenzene, thus producing a polymer having a hybridized skeleton (poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB)). This polymer exhibits a porous structure and fluorescence and colorimetric sensing abilities toward Cu2+ ions. More importantly, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) exhibits high skeleton stability in acidic and alkaline environments. By reintroducing zinc ions, the chemical properties, porous structure, and the sensing ability are regenerated after being destroyed in an acidic or alkaline environment, thus demonstrating its recyclability.

ce:section.ce:para: The selective detection and efficient separation of transition metal ions have become increasingly important because of their vital role in biological processes and their high toxicity [1–4]. Among these, Cu2+ ions are one of the most relevant divalent metal ions in the human body and major pollutants in wastewater streams [5–8]. In particular, excessive Cu2+ ion concentration in organisms may severely damage their brain, liver, and central nervous system [9,10]. Several methods have been used to detect Cu2+ ions, including inductively coupled plasma–atomic emission/mass spectrometry, surface plasma resonance detection, atomic absorption spectroscopy, and electrochemical methods [11–13]. In this context, fluorometric and colorimetric sensors have become increasingly popular because of their high sensitivity, simplicity, and facile reactivity [14–16].

Porous coordination polymers (PCPs), particularly metal–organic frameworks (MOFs), are a family of porous polymers whose skeleton is connected by coordination bonds. A variety of functional metal ions and ligands can be introduced into PCPs, enabling applications such as capture and separation of gases [17,18], heterogeneous catalysis [19], and energy storage [20,21]. In this regard, the sensing of metal ions using PCPs has received considerable attention in recent years [22–29]. The sensing ability of metal ions generally originates from their inner filter effect, the interaction of metal ions with ligands in PCPs, or the exchange of metal ions, which hinder the energy transfer, or completely alter the coordinated structure. Extensive efforts have been dedicated to designing luminescent MOFs for Cu2+-ion detection [30–34]. However, a large number of PCPs and MOFs often suffer from low stability in acidic or alkaline environments, limiting their application. To overcome this drawback, new frameworks have been designed to enhance their stability. For example, the hard and soft acid and base theory was used to design and synthesize various zirconium-based MOFs [35–37] that exhibit high stability over a wide pH range. In addition, MOFs containing N-heterocycle compounds as ligands exhibited high stability at a high pH value [38]. Nevertheless, the stability of these MOFs at low pH was not addressed.

Porous organic polymers (POPs) are another family of porous polymers [39], including hypercrosslinked polymers [40,41], polymers of intrinsic microporosity [42,43], and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [44,45]. Unlike PCPs, the skeletons of POPs are created by covalent bonds. This structural feature confers POPs with relatively high stability toward acids or alkalis; Alternatively, they exhibit lower levels of selectivity than PCPs because general ligands can interact with multiple metal ions. Although POPs, in particular COFs, have demonstrated fluorometric sensing ability toward several heavy metals [46–48], studies on their sensing ability toward Cu ions are still very limited.

The development of stable PCPs over a wide pH range is still challenging. Nevertheless, in spite of this stability issue, PCPs could be reused if their original structures and properties could be regenerated after being destroyed in acidic or alkaline environment. Unfortunately, little attention has been paid to this possibility. Motivated by this, we propose in this work a new concept to realize the recyclability of PCPs by incorporating a POP framework into that of PCPs. In this regard, numerous studies have focused on the preparation of MOFs/COFs nanocomposites [49]that exhibit core/shell nanostructures [50] or multilayered structures. Conversely, to the best of our knowledge, the hybridization of POP and PCP frameworks at a molecular scale has not been reported yet.

To achieve the hybridization of POP and PCP frameworks, we propose a strategy called “coordination copolymerization.” Unlike the conventional strategy used to prepare PCPs through direct polymerization via a coordination reaction, the “coordination-copolymerization” strategy is based on the copolymerization of coordination compounds with conventional polymerizable monomers, such as divinyl monomers. To develop this strategy, the copolymerization of a coordination compound (4-vinylpyridine zinc, ZnVP2) and a typical crosslinker (divinylbenzene, DVB) was performed to fabricate a new POP/PCP-hybridized framework; the formation of this framework was confirmed experimentally. The new poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) material exhibits a porous structure and fluorescence and colorimetric sensing ability toward Cu2+ ions. This multifunctional sensing ability is difficult to achieve in most of the current sensors. Moreover, owing to the formation of the hybridized framework, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) is characterized by high skeleton stability over a wide pH range and is recyclable. The chemical properties, porous structure, and the sensing ability of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) were investigated in detail.

4-Vinylpyridine and Divinylbenzene (DVB) was purchased from Aladdin (China). Dichorol-zinc (ZnCl2) were purchased from West Long chemical Co. Ltd (China). Other chemicals and solvents were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co. Ltd (China). DVB was subsequently treated by NaOH, washed with water and then purified by elution from Al2O3 column. Other chemicals were used without further purification. Di(4-vinylpyridine)-dichloro-zinc (C7NH8ZnCl2, ZnVP2) was synthesized according to the method as reported in our group [51].

In a single-neck reaction flask, ZnVP2 (2.36 g, 5.88 mmol), DVB (0.764 g, 5.88 mmol), BPO (15 mg) and dioxane (150 mL) were added, and the solution was evacuated three times under a nitrogen atmosphere. Once ZnVP2 was completely dissolved, the temperature was raised to 70 °C. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 12 h. The solution was centrifuged, affording white powders. The powders were repeatedly centrifuged in THF and ethanol for three times. The solid product was then dried under vacuum at 50 °C.

Poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was stirred in a 10 % HCl solution (w:v = 1:20) for 12 h and then treated with a 5 % NaOH solution to neutralize the polymers. The polymers were filtrated, washed with deionized water and dried. Finally, they were stirred with a zinc chloride in a certain ratio (pyridyl: Zn = 2:1) in 1,4-dioxane at 70 °C for 12 h to obtain a cyclic coordination polymer-- Poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB)-OH-Zn.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements in the frequency range of 400–4000 cm−1 were conducted using a Nicolet FTIR 5700 spectrophotometer at room temperature, and the sample films were prepared by casting the corresponding solutions on KBr plates. Thermogravimetric analyses were performed using a simultaneous SDT Q160 thermal analyzer, TA Instruments, USA at temperatures ranging from room temperature to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under an N2 atmosphere. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured using a Micromeritics NOVA 3000 system at 77 K. The samples were degassed at 100 °C for 1 h prior to analysis. X-ray diffraction spectra were recorded using an X'Pert pro X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical B.V.) with copper Kα radiation in the 2θ range of 3°–80°. Scanning electron microscopy was performed using a vacuum field emission scanning electron microscope MAIA3LMU (TESCAN Brno.s.r.o.). The zeta potential of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) particles was measured using a zeta potentiometer model ZetaPALS (Brookhaven, USA).

To evaluate the sensing ability of the poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) material toward metal ions, 10 mg of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was dispersed in 10 mL of an aqueous solution comprising metal ions (Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, and Pb2+) with a concentration of 100 ppm. After 24 h, the solution was sonicated. Then, 2 mL of this solution was immediately extracted and subjected to fluorescence spectroscopy measurement, which was conducted on a spectrofluorometer model F-4600 (Hitachi, Japan). For the competition experiments, other cations were added to the Cu2+ solution in a mole ratio of 1:1. The concentration of Cu2+ was also controlled to 100 ppm. The mole ratio of the solution containing Cu2+, Zn2+, and Ni2+ was controlled to be 1:1:1. To evaluate the applicability of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB), mine wastewater was collected and used as the real sample; 5 mL of wastewater was collected and then diluted 100 times with 5% H2NO3. The concentration of Cu2+ was measured through atomic absorption spectroscopy using an atomic absorption spectrometer (PE, USA).

For conventional POPs and PCPs, the skeletons were generally created by coordinated and covalent reaction (Scheme 1 a and b). In this work, we propose a “coordination-copolymerization” strategy as shown in Scheme 1c. Coordination compounds with reactive functional groups were constructed at first. Then, they are allowed to further copolymerize with another di/multi-functional covalent compound, in the way similar with that of POPS, to form hybridized skeleton. According this idea, we designed a model reaction between ZnVP2 and DVB (Scheme 1d). The copolymerization of ZnVP2 and DVB was conducted via the way of free radical polymerization. To make a comparison, homopolymer of ZnVP2 (poly(ZnVP2)) without introducing DVB was also synthesized.

The structure of as-resulted copolymer, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB), was investigated by Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). It can be seen that the spectrum of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) is almost identical with that of poly(ZnVP2). The absorption bands at 1557, 1503, and 1430 cm−1 are assigned to the characteristic vibration of pyridine ring. The new band at 1031 cm-1 is ascribed to the asymmetric ring breathing of pyridine in the complexes. The absorption bands at 1069 and 833 cm-1 are assigned to the in-plane and out-of-plane rings CH bending. The peaks at 1219 cm-1 is assigned to the CC in plane bending. The absorption band at 1617 cm-1 corresponds to the coordination bond [52,53], confirming the presence of N-heterocycle-metal coordination skeleton (Fig. 1 a). The band at 992 cm-1, which is attributed to the vinyl group, is not found, indicating successful polymerization of the vinyl groups. No refraction peak is found in XRD pattern of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB), which is indicative of an amorphous structure like poly(ZnVP2) (Fig. 1b). The amorphous structure should be attributed to the polymerization which inhibited the crystallization of ZnVP2.

For poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB), more worthy noted issue is whether the copolymerization existed. To address this issue, we investigated the change of structure of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) with acids. The dissolving or swelling of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was not observed at all pH range (Fig. 2 b). In comparison, poly(ZnVP2) began to be dissolved at pH < 3 (Fig. 2a) because the pyridine-metal coordination bonds will be broken to form pyridine chloride and then dis-crosslinking took place. As is well-known, polyDVB is highly stable no matter at acidic or alkali environment owing to its covalent network. Thus, above results imply that the framework of covalent bonds was possibly introduced into that of coordination bonds in poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB). Zeta potential of Poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was measured in the range of 14∼18 mV, indicating a positive charge on Poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB). The positive charge could be attributed to the presence of Zn2+ in Poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB).

Approximately 47 % of the total fluorescence intensity of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was quenched by only 10 μM of Cu2+ in water. Only slight quenching was observed when other cations (such as Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Sr2+, and Pb2+) were added, indicating higher selectivity of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) toward Cu2+ (see Figs. 7 a). The data of quenching efficiency is extracted and shown in Fig. 7b. The quenching efficiency of Cu2+ reaches to around 80 %, while that of other cations is commonly below 30 %. To evaluate the application of poly (ZnVP2-co-DVB), a mine waste water was extracted as real samples. In prior to measurement, the waste water was diluted by 100 times. The measured Cu2+ concentration for this waste water from atomic absorption spectroscopy was found at 6.2 ppm. In the addition of poly (ZnVP2-co-DVB), the fluorescent intensity of the waste water was measured at 1160 and then F-F0=-2140. According to the equation given by the fitted linear curve of F-F0 versus Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 6b), the Cu2+ concentration was calculated as 8.8 ppm. The values measured by the two methods are close to each other.

As shown in Fig. 2a, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) exhibited stability no matter at low or high pH values. SEM image (Fig. 10 b) further showed no appreciable change of the morphology for poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) after treating at pH = 2. N2 adsorption experimental result indicate that the porous structure was also preserved under pH = 2. Above results revealed that the incorporation of covalent framework via “coordination-copolymerization” strategy can effectively improve skeleton stability of coordination polymers. However, in fact, the framework of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was destroyed with acids as shown in FTIR spectra (Fig. 3). Herein, we further investigated the recyclability of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB). After treating at pH = 2, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was treated at pH = 8 to reform pyridine groups. Then, ZnCl2 was incorporated to recover the coordination structure of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB). Fig. 3 showed the FTIR spectrum of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) after recycling. It can be seen that the adsorption band of coordination bond at 1615 cm−1 re-appears, revealing that the coordination structure is recovered. In addition, SEM image (Fig. 10c) indicated that the morphology of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) remained almost unchanged after recycling for three times.

The porous structure and sensing ability of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) after recycling were further investigated. Fig. 11 c showed that the recycled poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) preserved porous structure. BET area is 84.3 m2/g. Fig. 12 c showed that the selectively sensing ability of poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) was also recovered after recycling for three times. Moreover, it should be noted that after treated at pH = 2 and subsequently at pH = 8, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) failed to exhibit fluorescent toward Cu2+. This result further revealed that poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) actually experienced the structure destruction. This result also indicated the crucial role of coordination structure to result in the responsive fluorescent properties.

Herein, we proposed the “coordination-copolymerization” strategy to form a POP/PCP-hybridized framework. A coordination compound, ZnVP2, was copolymerized with a classical crosslinking agent (DVB) to introduce a covalent framework into a coordination polymer. The as-prepared copolymer exhibited a porous structure with a BET area of 89.2 m2 g−1, and selective luminescence and colorimetric sensing property toward Cu2+ ions. The detection limit for Cu2+ in the aqueous solution was found to be greatly lower than that in drinking water, and the detection was comparable to most of the current sensors. The measured concentration of Cu2+ from the fluorescent intensity was almost identical to that measured through atomic absorption spectroscopy. Owing to the POP/PCP-hybridized framework, poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) exhibited good skeleton stability over a wide pH range. Although poly(ZnVP2-co-DVB) lost its the sensing ability in acidic environments, it could be recovered by reintroducing Zn2+. The sensing ability and porous structure of the polymer can be preserved after it is recycled thrice. Overall, this study provides a new insight for the design of a series of porous materials exhibiting sensing ability and recyclability.

lili Liu: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Yuanliang Huang: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yang Xiong: Investigation. Quanli Zhang: Investigation. Junxiao Yang: Project administration. Yawen Huang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: L.L. Liu and Y.L. Huang contributed equally on this work. The authors acknowledge the support and foundation from Longshan academic talent research supporting program of Southwest University of Science and Technology (18LZXT02), Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2020YJ0333) and Academic Team Project of state Key Laboratory of Environmental-friendly Energy Materials (17fksy0102).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Y. Xiong is a Master candidate in the Southwest University of Science and Technology, major in Material Science and Engineering. He received his Bachelor's degrees in Material Science and Engineering from Southwest University of Science and Technology, China, in 2016. His current research interest includes low-dielectric materials and porous polymeric materials.

Y.W. Huang is a professor in the Southwest University of Science and Technology. He received his Doctor's degrees in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering from Shanghai Jiaotong University, China, in 2009. His current research interest includes porous polymeric materials and self-healing polymers.

L.L. Liu is an assistant professor in the Southwest University of Science and Technology. She received her Doctor's degrees in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering from Harbin Industrial University, China, in 2009. Her current research interest includes self-healing polymers and porous polymeric materials.
